state_snapshot.raw_text:
**August 21, 1858**

It was dry and dusty, between 10,000 and 12,000 people were in attendance when the debate began at 2:00 p.m. There were no seats or bleachers.

Douglas charged Lincoln with trying to “abolitionize” the Whig and Democratic Parties. He also charged Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Republican Party in 1854. Douglas accused Lincoln of taking the side of the common enemy in the Mexican War. Douglas also said Lincoln wanted to make Illinois “a free Negro colony.” Douglas asked Lincoln seven questions.

Lincoln during his turn did not respond to the questions and was on the defensive denying the allegations Douglas had made. Lincoln charged Douglas with trying to nationalize slavery.

In his rebuttal Douglas concentrated on the charge that Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Republican Party in 1854.

Source: Neely, Mark E. Jr. 1982. _The Abraham Lincoln Encyclopedia_. New York: Da Capo Press, Inc.

Full text of the Ottawa Debate follows.

**Mr. Douglas' Speech**

Ladies and gentlemen: I appear before you to-day for the purpose of discussing the leading political topics which now agitate the public mind. By an arrangement between Mr. Lincoln and myself, we are present here to-day for the purpose of having a joint discussion, as the representatives of the two great political parties of the State and Union, upon the principles in issue between those parties and this vast concourse of people, shows the deep feeling which pervades the public mind in regard to the questions dividing us.

Prior to 1854 this country was divided into two great political parties, known as the Whig and Democratic parties. Both were national and patriotic, advocating principles that were universal in their application. An old line Whig could proclaim his principles in Louisiana and Massachusetts alike. Whig principles had no boundary sectional line, they were not limited by the Ohio river, nor by the Potomac, nor by the line of the free and slave States, but applied and were proclaimed wherever the Constitution ruled or the American flag waved over the American soil. (Hear him, and three cheers.) So it was, and so it is with the great Democratic party, which, from the days of Jefferson until this period, has proven itself to be the historic party of this nation. While the Whig and Democratic parties differed in regard to a bank, the tariff, distribution, the specie circular and the sub-treasury, they agreed on the great slavery question which now agitates the Union. I say that the Whig party and the Democratic party agreed on this slavery question, while they differed on those matters of expediency to which I have referred. The Whig party and the Democratic party jointly adopted the Compromise measures of 1850 as the basis of a proper and just solution of this slavery question in all its forms. Clay was the great leader, with Webster on his right and Cass on his left, and sustained by the patriots in the Whig and Democratic ranks, who had devised and enacted the Compromise measures of 1850.

In 1851, the Whig party and the Democratic party united in Illinois in adopting resolutions indorsing and approving the principles of the Compromise measures of 1850, as the proper adjustment of that question. In 1852, when the Whig party assembled in Convention at Baltimore for the purpose of nominating a candidate for the Presidency, the first thing it did was to declare the Compromise measures of 1850, in substance and in principle, a suitable adjustment of that question. (Here the speaker was interrupted by loud and long continued applause.) My friends, silence will be more acceptable to me in the discussion of these questions than applause. I desire to address myself to your judgment, your understanding, and your consciences, and not to your passions or your enthusiasm. When the Democratic Convention assembled in Baltimore in the same year, for the purpose of nominating a Democratic candidate for the Presidency, it also adopted the compromise measures of 1850 as the basis of Democratic action. Thus you see that up to 1853-'54, the Whig party and the Democratic party both stood on the same platform with regard to the slavery question. That platform was the right of the people of each State and each Territory to decide their local and domestic institutions for themselves, subject only to the federal constitution.

During the session of Congress of 1853-'54, I introduced into the Senate of the United States a bill to organize the Territories of Kansas and Nebraska on that principle which had been adopted in the compromise measures of 1850, approved by the Whig party and the Democratic party in Illinois in 1851, and endorsed by the Whig party and the Democratic party in national convention in 1852. In order that there might be no misunderstanding in relation to the principle involved in the Kansas and Nebras

analysis_tasks.task_1: Fact extraction: Douglas frames himself as defender of national parties and the 1850 compromise while using associative accusations and patriotic appeals to portray Lincoln as a radical corrupter. Lincoln responds def

identified_arguments.arg_1: Douglas frames the debate as a contest between two historic, national parties (Whig and Democratic) with shared principles. [quote: "Prior to 1854 this country was divided into two great political parties, known as the Whig and Democ"]
identified_arguments.arg_2: Douglas asserts both parties were 'national and patriotic' and their principles were universally applicable. [quote: "Both were national and patriotic, advocating principles that were universal in their application."]
identified_arguments.arg_3: Douglas accuses Lincoln of trying to transform (‘‘abolitionize’’) the Whig and Democratic parties. [quote: "Douglas charged Lincoln with trying to “abolitionize” the Whig and Democratic Parties."]
identified_arguments.arg_4: Douglas accuses Lincoln of having been present at the drafting of a radical Republican 'abolitionist' platform in 1854. [quote: "Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Re"]
identified_arguments.arg_5: Douglas accuses Lincoln of siding with the common enemy during the Mexican War (an attack on patriotism). [quote: "Douglas accused Lincoln of taking the side of the common enemy in the Mexican War."]
identified_arguments.arg_6: Douglas portrays the pre-1854 Whig and Democratic consensus on the Compromise of 1850 as the appropriate solution to the slavery question. [quote: "The Whig party and the Democratic party jointly adopted the Compromise measures of 1850 as the basis"]
identified_arguments.arg_7: Lincoln responds defensively, denying Douglas' allegations and accusing Douglas of trying to nationalize slavery. [quote: "Lincoln charged Douglas with trying to nationalize slavery."]
identified_arguments.arg_8: Douglas uses audience-address and patriotic imagery to frame the debate as important and broadly relevant. [quote: "this vast concourse of people, shows the deep feeling which pervades the public mind in regard to th"]

fallacy_1.justification: Douglas implies Lincoln is culpable because he was 'present' at a radical platform's drafting, equating presence with endorsement. [quote: "Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Re"]

fallacy_2.justification: Framing Lincoln as wanting Illinois to be 'a free Negro colony' exaggerates or distorts his position to provoke fear. [quote: "Lincoln wanted to make Illinois “a free Negro colony.”"]

fallacy_3.justification: Attacking Lincoln for 'taking the side of the common enemy in the Mexican War' targets character/patriotism rather than policy. [quote: "Douglas accused Lincoln of taking the side of the common enemy in the Mexican War."]

fallacy_4.justification: Charging Lincoln with trying to 'abolitionize' the major parties portrays his influence as a dangerous, corrosive threat without detailed proof. [quote: "Douglas charged Lincoln with trying to “abolitionize” the Whig and Democratic Parties."]

fallacy_5.justification: Asserting Whig and Democratic agreement on slavery and the Compromise of 1850 glosses over intra-party divisions and regional differences. [quote: "The Whig party and the Democratic party jointly adopted the Compromise measures of 1850 as the basis"]

fallacy_6.justification: Asking 'seven questions' can be used to force answers that assume contested facts, a tactic to corner an opponent. [quote: "Douglas asked Lincoln seven questions."]

extracts.source_quote: Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Republican Party in 1854.

extracts.content: Douglas claims Lincoln sided with the enemy in the Mexican War.
extracts.source_quote: Douglas accused Lincoln of taking the side of the common enemy in the Mexican War.

extracts.content: Douglas claims Lincoln wanted to make Illinois a 'free Negro colony.'
extracts.source_quote: Lincoln wanted to make Illinois “a free Negro colony.”

extracts.content: Douglas claims the Whig and Democratic parties historically agreed on the slavery question and supported the Compromise of 1850.
extracts.source_quote: The Whig party and the Democratic party jointly adopted the Compromise measures of 1850 as the basis of a proper and just solution...

extracts.content: Narrative claim: Douglas asked Lincoln seven questions as a debate tactic.
Narrative claim: Lincoln did not answer the questions and defended himself by denying Douglas' charges.
extracts.source_quote: Lincoln during his turn did not respond to the questions and was on the defensive denying the allegations Douglas had made.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Douglas frames the debate as a contest between two historic, national parties (Whig and Democratic) with shared principles.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Distinguish the party label from internal reality: while Whig and Democratic organizations were national in name, each contained deep regional factions and conflicting commitments (especially over slavery and economic policy). Framing the contest as between two unified, principle-sharing national parties erases those substantive divisions and misleads about what the debate was actually about.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Douglas asserts both parties were 'national and patriotic' and their principles were universally applicable.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Reformulate Douglas's claim as the stronger, testable assertion that a single set of principles applied uniformly across the country. That reformulation collapses under evidence: party platforms and practices were often tailored to local interests and contradictory on key issues (e.g., slavery extension), so the idea of universally applicable party principles is unsustainable.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Douglas accuses Lincoln of trying to transform (‘‘abolitionize’’) the Whig and Democratic parties.
counter_arguments.counter_content: If merely advocating limits on slavery or moral opposition to its spread counts as attempting to 'abolitionize' entire parties, then any reformer who seeks change would be accused of subverting party identity—an absurd standard that would outlaw ordinary political persuasion and freeze parties in perpetuity.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Douglas accuses Lincoln of having been present at the drafting of a radical Republican 'abolitionist' platform in 1854.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Counter-example: the coalitions and meetings of 1854 brought together a wide range of opponents to the Kansas–Nebraska Act, many of them moderate Whigs or Free-Soilers who opposed extension of slavery without endorsing immediate abolition. Being associated with or present at anti–Kansas–Nebraska gatherings therefore does not prove Lincoln authored or embraced a radical abolitionist platform.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Douglas accuses Lincoln of siding with the common enemy during the Mexican War (an attack on patriotism).
counter_arguments.counter_content: Concede that Lincoln criticized some policies during the Mexican War, but pivot: principled dissent about unconstitutional or expansionist measures is itself a form of patriotism aimed at defending the republic's principles. Moreover, Lincoln's subsequent public service and emphasis on preserving the Union demonstrate his civic loyalty rather than disloyalty.

exchanges.point: Douglas: Prior to 1854 the country was divided into two great, national, patriotic parties (Whig and Democratic) advocating universally applicable principles.
exchanges.rebuttal: Lincoln: Denies Douglas's framing and pivots to a substantive policy charge, accusing Douglas of trying to nationalize slavery — shifting the focus from appeals to past unity to the present policy stakes.

exchanges.point: Douglas: Accuses Lincoln of trying to 'abolitionize' the Whig and Democratic parties (painting Lincoln as radicalizing established parties).
exchanges.rebuttal: Lincoln: Responds defensively, denying the allegation and counter-accusing Douglas of promoting national expansion of slavery rather than defending national unity.

exchanges.point: Douglas: Claims Lincoln was present when a very radical 'abolitionist' type Republican platform was written in 1854 (implying Lincoln's complicity with extreme positions).
exchanges.rebuttal: Lincoln: Denies the implication and rejects the characterization, framing Douglas's allegation as a misrepresentation designed to discredit him.

exchanges.point: Douglas: Accuses Lincoln of siding with the common enemy in the Mexican War, an attack on Lincoln's patriotism to undermine credibility.
exchanges.rebuttal: Lincoln: Implicitly rebuts by redirecting the debate to concrete policy (the nationalization of slavery) and by contesting Douglas's use of personal attacks; the provided excerpts show Lincoln emphasizing substantive stakes rather than engaging the patriotism charge directly.

**August 21, 1858**

It was dry and dusty, between 10,000 and 12,000 people were in attendance when the debate began at 2:00 p.m. There were no seats or bleachers.

Douglas charged Lincoln with trying to “abolitionize” the Whig and Democratic Parties. He also charged Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Republican Party in 1854. Douglas accused Lincoln of taking the side of the common enemy in the Mexican War. Douglas also said Lincoln wanted to make Illinois “a free Negro colony.” Douglas asked Lincoln seven questions.

Lincoln during his turn did not respond to the questions and was on the defensive denying the allegations Douglas had made. Lincoln charged Douglas with trying to nationalize slavery.

In his rebuttal Douglas concentrated on the charge that Lincoln had been present when a very radical “abolitionist” type platform had been written by the Republican Party in 1854.

Source: Neely, Mark E. Jr. 1982. _The Abraham Lincoln Encyclopedia_. New York: Da Capo Press, Inc.

Full text of the Ottawa Debate follows.

**Mr. Douglas' Speech**

Ladies and gentlemen: I appear before you to-day for the purpose of discussing the leading political topics which now agitate the public mind. By an arrangement between Mr. Lincoln and myself, we are present here to-day for the purpose of having a joint discussion, as the representatives of the two great political parties of the State and Union, upon the principles in issue between those parties and this vast concourse of people, shows the deep feeling which pervades the public mind in regard to the questions dividing us.

Prior to 1854 this country was divided into two great political parties, known as the Whig and Democratic parties. Both were national and patriotic, advocating principles that were universal in their application. An old line Whig could proclaim his principles in Louisiana and Massachusetts alike. Whig principles had no boundary sectional line, they were not limited by the Ohio river, nor by the Potomac, nor by the line of the free and slave States, but applied and were proclaimed wherever the Constitution ruled or the American flag waved over the American soil. (Hear him, and three cheers.) So it was, and so it is with the great Democratic party, which, from the days of Jefferson until this period, has proven itself to be the historic party of this nation. While the Whig and Democratic parties differed in regard to a bank, the tariff, distribution, the specie circular and the sub-treasury, they agreed on the great slavery question which now agitates the Union. I say that the Whig party and the Democratic party agreed on this slavery question, while they differed on those matters of expediency to which I have referred. (Here the speaker was interrupted by loud and long continued applause.) My friends, silence will be more acceptable to me in the discussion of these questions than applause. I desire to address myself to your judgment, your understanding, and your consciences, and not to your passions or your enthusiasm. When the Democratic Convention assembled in Baltimore in the same year, for the purpose of nominating a Democratic candidate for the Presidency, it also adopted the compromise measures of 1850 as the basis of Democratic action. state_snapshot.raw_text:
MY FELLOW-CITIZENS: When a man hears himself somewhat misrepresented, it provokes him-at least, I find it so with myself; but when misrepresentation becomes very gross and palpable, it is more apt to amuse him. The first thing I see fit to notice, is the fact that Judge Douglas alleges, after running through the history of the old Democratic and the old Whig parties, that Judge Trumbull and myself made an arrangement in 1854, by which I was to have the place of Gen. Shields in the United States Senate, and Judge Trumbull was to have the place of Judge Douglas. Now, all I have to say upon that subject is, that I think no man-not even Judge Douglas-can prove it, _because it is not true_. [Cheers.] I have no doubt he is "_conscientious_" in saying it. [Laughter.] As to those resolutions that he took such a length of time to read, as being the platform of the Republican party in 1854, I say I never had anything to do with them, and I think Trumbull never had. [Renewed laughter.] Judge Douglas cannot show that either of us ever did have anything to do with them. I believe _this_ is true about those resolutions: There was a call for a Convention to form a Republican party at Springfield, and I think that my friend, Mr. Lovejoy, who is here upon this stand, had a hand in it. I think this is true, and I think if he will remember accurately, he will be able to recollect that he tried to get me into it, and I would not go in. [Cheers and laughter.] I believe it is also true that I went away from Springfield when the Convention was in session, to attend court in Tazewell county. It is true they did place my name, though without authority, upon the committee, and afterward wrote me to attend the meeting of the committee, but I refused to do so, and I never had anything to do with that organization. This is the plain truth about all that matter of the resolutions.

Now, about this story that Judge Douglas tells of Trumbull bargaining to sell out the old Democratic party, and Lincoln agreeing to sell out the old Whig party, I have the means of _knowing_ about that; Judge Douglas cannot have; and I know there is no substance to it whatever. Yet I have no doubt he is "_conscientious_" about it. I know that after Mr. Lovejoy got into the Legislature that winter, he complained of me that I had told all the old Whigs of his district that the old Whig party was good enough for them, and some of them voted against him because I told them so. Now, I have no means of totally disproving such charges as this which the Judge makes. A man cannot prove a negative, but he has a right to claim that when a man makes an affirmative charge, he must offer some proof to show the truth of what he says. I certainly cannot introduce testimony to show the negative about things, but I have a right to claim that if a man says he _knows_ a thing, then he must show _how_ he knows it. I always have a right to claim this, and it is not satisfactory to me that he may be "conscientious" on the subject. [Cheers and Laughter.]

Now, gentlemen, I hate to waste my time on such things, but in regard to that general Abolition tilt that Judge Douglas makes, when he says that I was engaged at that time in selling out and abolitionizing the old Whig party-I hope you will permit me to read a part of a printed speech that I made then at Peoria, which will show altogether a different view of the position I took in that contest of 1854.

VOICE-"Put on your specs."

MR. LINCOLN-Yes, sir, I am obliged to do so. I am no longer a young man. [Laughter.]

"This is the _repeal_ of the Missouri Compromise. The foregoing history may not be precisely accurate in every particular; but I am sure it is sufficiently so for all the uses I shall attempt to make of it, and in it we have before us, the chief materials enabling us to correctly judge whether the repeal of the Missouri Compromise is right or wrong.

"I think, and shall try to show, that it is wrong; wrong in its direct effect, letting slavery into Kansas and Nebraska-and wrong in its prospective principle, allowing it to spread to every other part of the wide world, where men can be found inclined to take it.

"This _declared_ indifference, but, as I must think, covert real zeal for the spread of slavery, I cannot but hate. I hate it because of the monstrous injustice of slavery itself. I hate it because it deprives our republican example of its just influence in the world-enables the enemies of free institutions, with plausibility, to taunt us as hypocrites-causes the real friends of freedom to doubt our sincerity, and especially because it forces so many really good men amongst ourselves into an open war with the very fundamental principles of civil liberty-criticizing the Declaration of Independence, and insisting that there is no right principle of action but _self-interest_.

"Before proceeding, let me say I think I have no prejudice against the Southern people. They are just what we would be in their situation. If

analysis_tasks.task_1: Fact extraction: Lincoln systematically denies Douglas's charge of a 1854 bargain, distances himself from the Republican resolutions, and demands that affirmative accusations be supported by proof. He combines factual

identified_arguments.arg_1: Denying the alleged 1854 arrangement with Trumbull and Douglas as false and unprovable. [quote: "no man-not even Judge Douglas-can prove it, _because it is not true_."]
identified_arguments.arg_2: Asserting that accusations require proof from the accuser; burden of proof lies with affirmative claims. [quote: "when a man makes an affirmative charge, he must offer some proof to show the truth of what he says."]
identified_arguments.arg_3: Rejecting involvement in the 1854 Republican platform and distancing himself from that organization. [quote: "I say I never had anything to do with them, and I think Trumbull never had."]

identified_arguments.arg_5: Asserting Douglas lacks the means to know about alleged bargain, claiming privileged knowledge to refute it. [quote: "Now, about this story... I have the means of _knowing_ about that; Judge Douglas cannot have; and I "]

fallacy_1.justification: Uses amusement and mockery to undermine the accusation rather than only addressing evidence. [quote: "it is more apt to amuse him."]

fallacy_2.justification: Asserts innocence partly on the lack of proof provided by the accuser; relying on absence of evidence to reject the claim. [quote: "A man cannot prove a negative, but he has a right to claim that when a man makes an affirmative char"]

extracts.content: The alleged arrangement between Lincoln and Trumbull in 1854 never happened.
extracts.source_quote: no man-not even Judge Douglas-can prove it, _because it is not true_.

extracts.content: Lincoln had nothing to do with the 1854 Republican resolutions or party organization.
extracts.source_quote: I say I never had anything to do with them, and I think Trumbull never had.

extracts.content: Mr. Lovejoy invited Lincoln to the Republican convention but Lincoln refused to join.
extracts.source_quote: he tried to get me into it, and I would not go in.

extracts.content: Lincoln left Springfield during the convention to attend court in Tazewell county.
extracts.source_quote: I went away from Springfield when the Convention was in session, to attend court in Tazewell county.

extracts.content: His name was placed on the committee without his authority and he declined to attend.
extracts.source_quote: they did place my name, though without authority, upon the committee, and afterward wrote me to attend the meeting... but I refused to do so

extracts.content: Some old Whigs voted against Lovejoy because Lincoln told them the old Whig party was good enough.
extracts.source_quote: he complained of me that I had told all the old Whigs of his district that the old Whig party was good enough for them, and some of them voted against him because I told them so.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Denying the alleged 1854 arrangement with Trumbull and Douglas as false and unprovable.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Lack of a single smoking-gun document does not make an arrangement impossible to establish; historians and fact-finders routinely infer agreements from contemporaneous correspondence, timing of actions, mutual benefits, and consistent witness testimony. Pointing to pattern evidence or corroborating statements from third parties can make the claim reasonably supported even if no letter explicitly names the bargain.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Asserting that accusations require proof from the accuser; burden of proof lies with affirmative claims.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Distinguish legal adjudication from political and historical judgment: while courts require proof beyond a defined standard, political accountability and historical inference operate on probabilities and shifting burdens where circumstantial evidence and the respondent's explanations both matter; insisting on a strict legal burden ignores the context where the accused must also rebut credible patterns of evidence.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Rejecting involvement in the 1854 Republican platform and distancing himself from that organization.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Reformulate the objection: even if he did not formally belong to the group, what matters for public judgment is whether his policies, speeches, or votes aligned with and materially advanced the platform; distancing himself on organizational grounds does not negate functional association if his actions effectively supported the platform's aims.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Explaining he did not participate because his name was placed without authority and he refused to join.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Concede that the placement may have been unauthorized, but pivot to the point that once his name circulated he bore responsibility to publicly correct it or disavow it; passive refusal after the fact looks like tacit acceptance and fails as a full defense unless he can show prompt, effective disavowal.

counter_arguments.original_argument: Asserting Douglas lacks the means to know about alleged bargain, claiming privileged knowledge to refute it.
counter_arguments.counter_content: Reduce this to absurdity: if only the alleged beneficiary could ever know of a bargain, then every denial by those same actors would be unchallengeable and no investigation could proceed; the claim would render inquiry impossible and allow anyone to immunize themselves by asserting exclusive knowledge.

trace.speaker: proponent
trace.argument: MY FELLOW-CITIZENS: When a man hears himself somewhat misrepresented, it provokes him-at least, I find it so with myself; but when misrepresentation becomes very gross and palpable, it is more apt to

formulas:
MY FELLOW-CITIZENS: When a man hears himself somewhat misrepresented, it provokes him-at least, I find it so with myself; but when misrepresentation becomes very gross and palpable, it is more apt to amuse him. The first thing I see fit to notice, is the fact that Judge Douglas alleges, after running through the history of the old Democratic and the old Whig parties, that Judge Trumbull and myself made an arrangement in 1854, by which I was to have the place of Gen. Shields in the United States Senate, and Judge Trumbull was to have the place of Judge Douglas. Now, all I have to say upon that subject is, that I think no man-not even Judge Douglas-can prove it, _because it is not true_. [Cheers.] I have no doubt he is "_conscientious_" in saying it. [Laughter.] As to those resolutions that he took such a length of time to read, as being the platform of the Republican party in 1854, I say I never had anything to do with them, and I think Trumbull never had. [Renewed laughter.] Judge Douglas cannot show that either of us ever did have anything to do with them. I believe _this_ is true about those resolutions: There was a call for a Convention to form a Republican party at Springfield, and I think that my friend, Mr. Lovejoy, who is here upon this stand, had a hand in it. I think this is true, and I think if he will remember accurately, he will be able to recollect that he tried to get me into it, and I would not go in. [Cheers and laughter.] I believe it is also true that I went away from Springfield when the Convention was in session, to attend court in Tazewell county. It is true they did place my name, though without authority, upon the committee, and afterward wrote me to attend the meeting of the committee, but I refused to do so, and I never had anything to do with that organization. This is the plain truth about all that matter of the resolutions.

Now, about this story that Judge Douglas tells of Trumbull bargaining to sell out the old Democratic party, and Lincoln agreeing to sell out the old Whig party, I have the means of _knowing_ about that; Judge Douglas cannot have; and I know there is no substance to it whatever. Yet I have no doubt he is "_conscientious_" about it. I know that after Mr. Lovejoy got into the Legislature that winter, he complained of me that I had told all the old Whigs of his district that the old Whig party was good enough for them, and some of them voted against him because I told them so. Now, I have no means of totally disproving such charges as this which the Judge makes. A man cannot prove a negative, but he has a right to claim that when a man makes an affirmative charge, he must offer some proof to show the truth of what he says. I certainly cannot introduce testimony to show the negative about things, but I have a right to claim that if a man says he _knows_ a thing, then he must show _how_ he knows it. I always have a right to claim this, and it is not satisfactory to me that he may be "conscientious" on the subject. [Cheers and Laughter.]

Now, gentlemen, I hate to waste my time on such things, but in regard to that general Abolition tilt that Judge Douglas makes, when he says that I was engaged at that time in selling out and abolitionizing the old Whig party-I hope you will permit me to read a part of a printed speech that I made then at Peoria, which will show altogether a different view of the position I took in that contest of 1854.

VOICE-"Put on your specs."

MR. LINCOLN-Yes, sir, I am obliged to do so. I am no longer a young man. [Laughter.]

"This is the _repeal_ of the Missouri Compromise. The foregoing history may not be precisely accurate in every particular; but I am sure it is sufficiently so for all the uses I shall attempt to make of it, and in it we have before us, the chief materials enabling us to correctly judge whether the repeal of the Missouri Compromise is right or wrong.

"I think, and shall try to show, that it is wrong; wrong in its direct effect, letting slavery into Kansas and Nebraska-and wrong in its prospective principle, allowing it to spread to every other part of the wide world, where men can be found inclined to take it.

"This _declared_ indifference, but, as I must think, covert real zeal for the spread of slavery, I cannot but hate. I hate it because of the monstrous injustice of slavery itself. I hate it because it deprives our republican example of its just influence in the world-enables the enemies of free institutions, with plausibility, to taunt us as hypocrites-causes the real friends of freedom to doubt our sincerity, and especially because it forces so many really good men amongst ourselves into an open war with the very fundamental principles of civil liberty-criticizing the Declaration of Independence, and insisting that there is no right principle of action but _self-interest_.

"Before proceeding, let me say I think I have no prejudice against the Southern people. They are just what we would be in their situation. If
